coredata.dc:title: Heavy atom effect boosts the achievement of ultra-efficient long afterglow room temperature phosphorescent carbon point composites: An enhanced approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jian
Chen, Ling
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have experienced rapid growth due to their enduring luminescence, high resolution, and other distinctive characteristics. Simultaneously, carbon dots (CDs) have gained popularity across various disciplines for their efficient luminescence, straightforward synthesis, and environmental friendliness. In this study, we successfully synthesized a range of high-performance carbon dot materials using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Through detailed morphological and photophysical characterizations, we discovered that these materials exhibited a remarkable phosphorescence quantum yield of up to 53.15 %, an impressive feat for both metal and non-metal carbon dot materials. Furthermore, we explored the enhancing effect of the heavy atom on ultra-efficient long afterglow RTP carbon dot composites and experimentally validated its effectiveness. Finally, we applied these materials to information encryption technology, offering novel ideas and methods for RTP material applications. This research not only opens new avenues for the development of carbon dot materials but also provides a promising direction and outlook for their future use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Long afterglow room temperature phosphorescent
Heavy atom effect
Ultra-efficient PLQY
Information encryption technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of MCD20, MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI.
(a) TEM images of MCD20. (b) HRTEM images of MCD20. (c) Size distribution of MCD20. (d) Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of MCD20 powder.
XPS (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, and (c) B 1s spectrum of MCD20. (c) XRD patterns of MCD20.

(a) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectrum of MCD20 aqueous solution. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MCD20 aqueous solution. (c) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (red line) of MCD20. (d) Afterglow excitation spectrum (blue line) and afterglow emission spectrum (yellow line) of MCD20.

(a) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of MCD20. (b) Excitation-dependent afterglow emission spectra of MCD20. (c) CIE coordinate diagram of MCD20. (d) Afterglow decay curves of MCD20 monitored with 365 nm excitation. (e) Corresponding photographs of MCD20 under conditions of sunlight, and 365 nm UV lamp on and off.
(a) Temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectrum of MCD20 under excitation at 365 nm. (b) Proposed mechanism of phosphorescence emission.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI, respectively. Excitation-dependent afterglow emission spectrum of (b) MCD20-KCl, (c) MCD20-KBr and (d) MCD20-KI. (e) Corresponding photographs of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI under conditions of sunlight, and 365 nm UV lamp on and off.
(a) Afterglow decay curves of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI. (b) XRD patterns of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI. (c) FT-IR spectra of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI.
Data encryption application of MCD20 and MCD20-KX powder.
Afterglow lifetimes of CDs under ambient conditions.
Summary of related photophysical properties data.

head.ce:title: Heavy atom effect boosts the achievement of ultra-efficient long afterglow room temperature phosphorescent carbon point composites: An enhanced approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have experienced rapid growth due to their enduring luminescence, high resolution, and other distinctive characteristics. Simultaneously, carbon dots (CDs) have gained popularity across various disciplines for their efficient luminescence, straightforward synthesis, and environmental friendliness. In this study, we successfully synthesized a range of high-performance carbon dot materials using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Through detailed morphological and photophysical characterizations, we discovered that these materials exhibited a remarkable phosphorescence quantum yield of up to 53.15 %, an impressive feat for both metal and non-metal carbon dot materials. Furthermore, we explored the enhancing effect of the heavy atom on ultra-efficient long afterglow RTP carbon dot composites and experimentally validated its effectiveness. Finally, we applied these materials to information encryption technology, offering novel ideas and methods for RTP material applications. This research not only opens new avenues for the development of carbon dot materials but also provides a promising direction and outlook for their future use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 21st century, room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials [1–3] have rapidly developed [4,5], primarily due to their enduring afterglow luminescence [6] and high resolution [7,8] as well as other unique characteristics. Researchers have been attracted to these materials as potential alternatives to fluorescence, thermal activation delayed fluorescence (TADF) and other widely used compounds in OLED [9], lighting and other fields. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) materials have gradually penetrated into various disciplines due to their high luminous efficiency, simple synthesis process [10], green environmental protection [11] and other unique properties [9,12–15]. Remarkable progress has been made in catalysis [16], anti-counterfeiting ink [17], programmable luminescent tags [18], fingerprinting [19],data encryption and decryption [20–22]. However, despite the long-term pursuit of efficient luminescence in carbon dot materials, the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) performance still falls behind their excellent fluorescence emission. Compared to mature and nearly 100 % luminous efficiency of traditional fluorescent materials, most prepared carbon dots and carbon dot composite materials exhibit a phosphorescence quantum yield of only about 10%–30 %, which hinders their practical application expansion [23,24].

Recently, Bai et al. [25] designed and constructed an efficient blue room temperature phosphorescent material through a simple molecular engineering strategy, obtaining an excellent long-afterglow material with a phosphorescent quantum efficiency of up to 50.17 %. George et al. [26] reported one of the remarkable quantum yields (about 41.8 %) in aqueous solution from a simple phthalimide phosphor at room temperature by employing an organic-inorganic supramolecular scaffold strategy. Therefore, The studies mentioned above suggest that stable and efficient room temperature phosphorescent materials can be achieved through rational design strategies and methods.

In this study, we designed and constructed a high-efficiency yellow-green room temperature phosphorescent carbon point composite with both luminous efficiency and afterglow lifetime through a simple synthesis method. The phosphorescent quantum efficiency is as high as 38.62 %, the afterglow lifetime is as long as 993.6 ms, and the continuous afterglow emission of 9 s can be visually observed. Due to the molten glass B2O3 obtained by the dehydration of boric acid at high temperatures, the matrix network with high rigidity can be used as a protective structure to inhibit the non-radiative decay process of carbon dots and promote the emission of RTP by limiting the vibration of molecules and insulating the quenching of oxygen. In addition, to explore the influence of heavy atom effect on the afterglow material and achieve more efficient phosphorescence quantum yield [27,28], the spin-orbit coupling effect was further introduced to enhance the absorption transition rate from S0 to T1 state and the intersystem crossing rate from S1 to T1 state, overcoming the internal competition between the photoluminescence lifetime and efficiency involved in the triple excited state. Three kinds of carbon point composites containing heavy atoms of chlorine, bromine and iodine were obtained by improving the phosphorescence generation and quantum yield. Under UV excitation at 365 nm, the phosphorescence quantum yields of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI are greatly improved, and the phosphorescence quantum yields of MCD20-KBr are as high as 53.15 %, which is higher than most non-metallic carbon dot room temperature phosphorescence afterglow materials reported so far. The ultra-long yellow-green light and yellow-light afterglow of nearly 1 s were achieved. This study may pave the way for high-performance persistence materials including information encryption, lighting and new displays to have more extensive applications in the future and provide basic evidence and application examples for CDs-based composites.

Both gallic acid and boric acid were purchased from Chongqing Taixin Chemical Co., Ltd., while potassium chloride, potassium bromide, and potassium iodide were purchased from Beijing InnoChem Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

20 mg gallic acid and 3 g boric acid (BA) were added into a beaker containing 40 mL deionized water. The mixture was dissolved by ultrasonication and heating. Then, cover the beaker with a layer of foil and react in the oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, grind the solid and obtain the white powder MCD20.

The preparation methods of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI materials are the same as above. Besides boric acid (3 g) and gallic acid (20 mg), 50 mg of KX (X = Cl, Br, I) was used as a precursor (see Fig. 1).

The morphology and structure of MCD20 were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), which revealed a uniform sphere-shaped structure. The high-resolution TEM images of MCD20 provided a clearer view of MCD20 (Fig. 2 a and b), showing distinct lattice fringes and an average diameter of 3.42 nm (Fig. 2c). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was further employed to determine the surface groups and chemical composition of MCD20, as shown in Fig. 2d. The FTIR spectra of MCD20 reveal that the enhancement at 3215 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH groups. The stretching vibration of the B–O bond of MCD20 is located in the distinct absorption band at 1438 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1193 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of the asymmetric oxygen atom B–O–H connected to the boron atom. Furthermore, a prominent absorption peak appears at 935 cm−1, which can be attributed to the newly formed B–C bond. These results suggest that a chemical reaction may have occurred during the high-temperature one-step hydrothermal reaction process, resulting in the formation of B–C and B–O bonds between CDs and boric acid.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of MCD20 further elaborates on its chemical composition and bonding structure. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be fitted with three binding energies of 284.8, 286.2 and 289.1 eV, corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–O and O–CO bonds, respectively. (Fig. 3 a). The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 3b) displayed two distinct characteristic peaks at 533.2 and 534.1 eV, attributed to C–O–C/C–O–B and O–CO/C–OH, respectively. The B1s XPS spectrum contains three components, which were fitted with the binding energies of 192.8, 193.7 and 194.8 eV, corresponding to BCO2, B2O3 and B–O (Fig. 3c), respectively. In addition, the XRD spectra of MCD20 showed characteristic peaks of boric acid at 14.6°, 27.7°, 30.3° and 40.4°, respectively (Fig. 3d). These results further confirmed the consistency between XPS analysis and FTIR results, indicating that the formation of strong covalent bonds occurred during the high-temperature hydrothermal reaction between CDs and BA, which effectively restricted the emission and vibration of CDs through a rigid environment constraint, thereby achieving efficient long-lasting luminescence emission.

The optical properties of the prepared MCD20 were characterized by UV-VIS, photoluminescence and Time-delay photoluminescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence spectrum of the MCD20 powder dissolved in water exhibited excitation-dependent features, with the emission peak gradually red-shifting to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased. Unfortunately, due to the rotation, vibration and relaxation of the molecules, no afterglow was observed in the aqueous solution of MCD20. As shown in Fig. 4b, the UV-VIS absorption spectrum of solid MCD20 shows three distinct absorption bands at 268, 388 and 540 nm respectively. The former one absorption band can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC, while the latter two originated from the n-π* transition of CO. The presence of CO can effectively promote spin-orbit coupling, thereby enhancing the intersystem crossing rate and triplet exciton generation. In addition, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of MCD20 has a distinct peak at 321 nm (solid blue line in Fig. 4c), corresponding to its absorption band at 300–400 nm, mainly attributed to the CO functional group of MCD20. The fluorescence emission spectrum of MCD20 shows two emission peaks at 416 and 525 nm, while the afterglow emission spectrum showed an optimal emission wavelength of 525 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength of 296 nm (Fig. 4c and d).

The fluorescence emission spectrum of MCD20 under different excitation were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a, MCD20 exhibited a significant excitation wavelength-dependent feature, with the emission peak shifting gradually from 400 nm to 480 nm as the excitation wavelength increased. The phosphorescence emission spectrum of MCD20 also exhibit excitation wavelength dependence characteristics similar to the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 5b). At an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, MCD20 exhibited white fluorescence and could be observed with a yellow-green afterglow that lasted for 9 s after removing the excitation source (Fig. 5e). The afterglow emission color of MCD20 was further confirmed by calculating CIE coordinates(Fig. 5c). Notably, that MCD20's afterglow quantum yield measured at 365 nm excitation is as high as 38.62 %, which achieves one of the highest levels among non-metallic room temperature phosphorescent CDs materials. In addition, the afterglow decay curve of MCD20 at 365 nm excitation could be fitted using a two-exponential function, with components of 156.04 ms and 1067.17 ms, resulting in an average afterglow lifetime of 993.6 ms (see in Fig. 5d and Table 1 ). These results indicate that the presence of triplet excited states may be responsible for the excitation-dependent features of MCD20, and the observation of solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra further confirms the RTP nature of the afterglow. In addition, the disappearance of the afterglow in solution suggests that the constructed afterglow materials in solution are easily affected by factors such as moisture and oxygen, resulting in quenching, while solid-state afterglow materials can effectively stabilize RTP performance. Detailed spectral and quantum yield data are listed in Table 2 .

To further verify the source of the afterglow emission, we measured the afterglow emission spectra of MCD20 at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 6 a–as the temperature increased from 80 K to 220 K, the intensity of the afterglow emission spectrum of MCD20 gradually decreased, thus confirming that the afterglow emission of MCD20 originates from phosphorescent radiation transition. Since low temperature can effectively inhibit molecular motion, but an increase in temperature can often accelerate molecular chromophore rotation and lattice vibration, quenching long-lived triplet excitons and leading to a decrease in phosphorescence intensity of MCD20 powder as the temperature increases. In addition, based on the above discussion, we propose a possible mechanism of MCD20 phosphorescence. Firstly, the CO bond can induce RTP in MCD20; secondly, boric acid is also crucial for the generation of RTP, as it can react with gallic acid to form new B–C and B–O covalent bonds. Strong covalent bond interactions and nanoscale confinement can inhibit triplet excited-state quenching and promote RTP emission. Therefore, electrons first transition from the ground state S0 to the excited state Sn, then return to the S1 state, and then undergo intersystem crossing process to lower to the T1 state [29–31]. Finally, non-radiative transition is suppressed and returns to the S0 state to produce room-temperature phosphorescence (Fig. 6b).

It is well known that the heavy atom effect can cause significant spin-orbit coupling phenomenon, thereby increasing the rate constant of electron spin transition, which can greatly enhance the performance of RTP. Inspired by this, in order to further understand the performance and phenomenon of the room-temperature phosphorescence activation of the prepared carbon dot-based composite materials, MCD20-KX (X = Cl, Br, I) were prepared by introducing KCl, KBr, KI into MCD20 to achieve efficient room-temperature phosphorescence RTP. Control experiments with the introduction of heavy atom effect were conducted for detailed morphological structure characterization and optical physical property exploration. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the UV–visible absorption spectra of MCD20-KX are similar to those of MCD20, and the PL of the composite materials can be excited by various excitation sources. In addition, the delayed emission spectra of MCD20-KX still maintain excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. 7b–d), i.e., they vary with changes in excitation wavelength. Under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp excitation, MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI exhibited white mixed light fluorescence emission, and displayed bright yellow-green long afterglow phenomenon after turning off the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 7e). Notably, after turning off the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, MCD20-KCl exhibited visually detectable room-temperature phosphorescence afterglow emission for more than 8 s, while MCD20-KBr and MCD20-KI displayed room-temperature phosphorescence afterglow emission for 7 s. Additionally, under 365 nm excitation, the delayed emission peak gradually redshifted. According to CIE color coordinates and emission color maps, the afterglow color gradually changed from yellow-green to yellow (Table 2, Fig. 5c).

In addition, MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI also exhibited long-lived luminescence similar to that of MCD20. The data obtained from double exponential fitting were 969.6, 954.4, and 952.4 ms, respectively (Fig. 8 a, Table 1). Furthermore, the afterglow quantum yields of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI were measured to be 43.3 %, 53.15 %, and 40.20 %, respectively. Compared to MCD20, the afterglow quantum yield of MCD20-KBr increased significantly to over 50 %, which is a promising result, whether in the metal or inorganic non-metal fields. Due to the heavy atom effect effectively promoting spin-orbit coupling and increasing transition rate constants, the optical performance of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI was significantly improved. However, for long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence, slower transitions are required. Therefore, the rapid transitions led to a decrease in the phosphorescence lifetimes of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI. On the other hand, the XRD spectra (Fig. 8b) and FTIR spectra (Fig. 8c) of MCD20-KCl, MCD20-KBr, and MCD20-KI were similar to those of MCD20. The XRD spectra exhibited distinct amorphous carbon and boric acid peaks, and their infrared spectra showed more distinct B–C covalent bond peaks, which confirmed the efficient protective effect of the boric acid matrix. In addition, the introduction of halogen ions generated a heavy atom effect that effectively enhanced phosphorescence emission and achieved controllable RTP.

Due to MCD20 and MCD20-KX's ultra-efficient phosphorescent quantum yields and RTP characteristics, with an extremely long afterglow lifetime of nearly 1 s, they show great application potential in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. As shown in Fig. 9 A, spread MCD20 powder evenly on the mold plate and lay out the pattern of “University”. The white fluorescence pattern of “University” can be observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light. After the UV lamp is turned off, the white fluorescence disappears, and the pattern changes from white to yellow-green.

In addition, because of the easy distinguishability of the room temperature phosphorescent colors of MCD20 and MCD20-KX, they can be used as a model to demonstrate the application prospects of these smart materials in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption security protection. As shown in Fig. 9B–a unique dual-mode switching encryption technique is implemented by loading MCD20 and MCD20-KI powder into a “padlock” mold. The powder in the square dashed box is made of MCD20 powder, and the rest of the “lock” is filled with MCD20-KI powder. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the patterns synthesized by MCD20 and MCD20-KI display bright white fluorescence, and the entire pattern appears white to the naked eye. So far, the first layer of information encryption is formed in the fluorescence emission. Because MCD20-KI displays a more redshifted yellow phosphorescence than MCD20, when the UV lamp is turned off, a different color appears in the square dashed box, and the pattern appears as “unlocked”. Thus, a second layer of encryption is formed in phosphorescence emission. In summary, the design and construction of the anti-counterfeiting information encryption based on MCD20 and MCD20-KI multifunctional composite materials show a unique mode of excitation information encryption with time resolution.

Compared to the luminescent performance of fluorescent materials, the quantum yield of phosphor materials is relatively low, which is a major concern in the research field. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop long-lived luminescent materials with high luminous efficiency and strong stability. In this work, high performance CDs materials were prepared by embedding gallic acid carbon points into boric acid matrix by a simple hydrothermal method. A strong interaction force such as B–C covalent bond is formed in the material, which limits and stabilizes the motion of the triplet exciton and thus inhibits the non-radiative relaxation process. The phosphorescence quantum yield of CDs is 38.62 %, the afterglow time is about 1 s, and the naked eye recognition time is 9 s. On this basis, through the introduction of heavy atoms of chlorine, bromine and iodine, the spin-orbit coupling of the molecules is used to improve the molecular transition rate, and the room temperature phosphorescence luminescence efficiency of the CDs composite is greatly improved. In particular, the long afterglow quantum yield of MCD20-KBr is up to 53.15 %, which is an exciting result in both the metal and inorganic non-metal fields. Furthermore, yellow-green materials with long afterglow are applied to information encryption technology, which provides a practical reference for high performance commercial applications. It provides a certain strategy for the future application of long-persistence room temperature phosphorescent carbon point matrix composites in the field of data encryption and anti-counterfeiting.
Jian Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Ling Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
